key_insight: SHIFT predicts survival directly from incomplete genomic profiles by using missingness-aware transformer attention instead of filling in absent genes.
clinical_relevance: This could make multi-center precision oncology models more robust when sequencing panels differ across hospitals, improving external transfer without discarding incomplete patients.
method: Represents each genomic feature separately and applies masked self-attention with a feature-availability mask.
Trains with variable-rate feature masking to simulate heterogeneous missingness patterns.
Uses a single model across differing feature sets without test-time imputation.
evidence_signal: In glioblastoma and lung squamous cell carcinoma, including external validation across multiple cohorts and severe panel mismatch, SHIFT generalized well and compared favorably with standard survival and imputation-based baselines.
watch_item: Performance may still depend on how well training data capture the real-world pattern of missingness and on careful validation before deployment across new sites.
paper_title: SHIFT: Survival Prediction from Incomplete and Heterogeneous Genomic Data
